ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani class (FOODON_00002700). No Hyderabadi-specific or chicken-specific biryani subclass exists; closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers biryani as a rice-based mixed dish. No Hyderabadi chicken-specific facet code available; closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for biryani or Hyderabadi chicken biryani as a food entity. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: dum cooking (slow steam cooking in sealed vessel)
main_ingredients: whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Hyderabadi Murgh Dum Biryani is approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 250 g of cooked basmati rice layered with 120–130 g of boneless chicken pieces and 20–30 g of garnish including fried onions, mint, and almond slivers.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Biryani — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition), composite dish entry; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for basmati rice (ID 169704) and roasted chicken (ID 171477) for micronutrient cross-check.
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) reports chicken biryani as a composite dish at approximately 130 kcal/100 g with macros consistent with a rice-and-chicken preparation. Hyderabadi dum biryani uses a richer preparation (ghee, almond, fried onions, yoghurt marinade) than a plain chicken biryani, so values are adapted upward to approximately 155 kcal/100 g to reflect the higher fat content from ghee and almond. Micronutrient values are cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy basmati rice and roasted chicken entries. This is an adapted estimate; exact Hyderabadi dum biryani values are not individually listed in ICMR-NIN.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for basmati rice biryani (GI ~57–60, tested food: chicken biryani with basmati rice).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database and the 2021 International GI Tables (Atkinson et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2021) report GI values for basmati rice-based biryani in the range of 54–60. A GI of 57 is used as the central estimate for Hyderabadi Murgh Dum Biryani, which uses basmati rice as the primary carbohydrate source. The higher fat and protein content from ghee, almond, and chicken in the dum preparation may modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain basmati rice (GI ~58). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (57 × 46.7 g) / 100 ≈ 26.6, where available carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 18.5 × 4 × 0.63 ≈ 46.7 g net carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg/100 g; ~1280 mg per restaurant serving of 400 g) from salt, spice blends, and ghee-based cooking
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute approximately 55–64% of the 2000 mg daily sodium target recommended by the Singapore Health Promotion Board.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~57) and high glycemic load per restaurant serving (~26.6 GL units) due to the large basmati rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit the serving to approximately 200 g (half a standard restaurant portion) to keep the glycemic load below 15 GL units per meal, and pair the dish with a non-starchy vegetable side to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee (~2.1 g/100 g; ~8.4 g per 400 g serving) and cholesterol (~38 mg/100 g; ~152 mg per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should consume Hyderabadi Murgh Dum Biryani occasionally and in portions of 200 g or less, as the ghee-based preparation contributes saturated fat that can raise LDL cholesterol when consumed regularly in large quantities.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hyderabadi Murgh Dum Biryani is a celebrated slow-cooked rice dish originating from the royal kitchens of Hyderabad, India, and is widely regarded as one of the most refined expressions of the biryani tradition. The dish is prepared using the dum method, in which marinated boneless chicken pieces and parboiled basmati rice are layered in a heavy-bottomed vessel or handi, sealed with dough or a tight-fitting lid, and cooked over a low flame so that the steam (dum) circulates internally, allowing the rice to absorb the aromatic spiced gravy without becoming mushy. The chicken is typically marinated in yoghurt, ginger-garlic paste, green chillies, and whole spices including cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and bay leaf. The dish is enriched with ghee, saffron-infused milk, golden fried onions (birista), fresh mint leaves, and almond slivers, which together give it a distinctive fragrance, a slightly nutty richness, and a golden-orange hue. The cooked rice grains are long, fluffy, and separate, while the chicken is tender and deeply spiced. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 155 kcal, 8.5 g protein, 5.8 g fat (of which 2.1 g is saturated, primarily from ghee), and 18.5 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is approximately 320 mg per 100 g, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension. The glycemic index of basmati rice-based biryani is approximately 57 (medium GI), and a standard 400 g restaurant serving carries a glycemic load of approximately 26.6 GL units, which is clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is naturally gluten-free and is commonly prepared as halal. In Singapore, Hyderabadi Murgh Dum Biryani is served at North Indian and Mughlai restaurants and is a popular lunch and dinner option, typically priced between SGD 17.90 and SGD 20.90 per portion.
common_user_queries: What is Hyderabadi Murgh Dum Biryani?
How many calories in chicken dum biryani?
Is chicken biryani good for diabetics?
What is the GI of biryani?
What makes Hyderabadi biryani different from other biryanis?

confidence_reason: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) contains a chicken biryani composite entry that provides a defensible macro baseline. Hyderabadi dum biryani is a richer preparation than a generic chicken biryani due to ghee, almond, and fried onion enrichment, so values have been adapted upward from the ICMR-NIN baseline. No exact Hyderabadi dum biryani entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA as a named dish; therefore confidence is medium rather than high. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2021 International GI Tables for basmati rice-based biryani.
notes: 1. The input food_name 'Hyderbadi Murgh Dum Biryani' contains a common misspelling ('Hyderbadi' instead of 'Hyderabadi'); the canonical spelling has been corrected in food_name. The misspelling is preserved as an English alias for retrieval purposes. 2. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken biryani composite entry (2017) with upward adjustment for the richer Hyderabadi dum preparation (ghee, almond, fried onion). Exact Hyderabadi dum biryani values are not individually listed in ICMR-NIN. 3. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice biryani data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) International GI Tables. 4. Serving size of 400 g is an estimate for a standard Singapore restaurant portion based on typical restaurant serving weights for biryani dishes. 5. Halal status is marked true as the dish is prepared with chicken and does not contain pork or alcohol by traditional recipe; however, individual restaurant certification should be verified. 6. Vitamin D not reported by ICMR-NIN for this dish category; set to -1. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002700 and FoodEx2 code A0FKP are the closest defensible ontology anchors; no Hyderabadi-specific subclass exists in either system.
